title: podman api stuck on container startup
body:

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

update: the real reason is here: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13081#issuecomment-1028719356

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

the problem was found  when I using the nomad-driver-podman, during nomad startup, it will recover stopped container via the rest api (`/v1.0.0/libpod/containers/{name}/start`).
and it also calls `/v1.0.0/libpod/info` and  the stats api `/v1.0.0/libpod/containers/{name}/stats?stream=false`

this three kinds of api will all stuck on system startup (it will not happend if it is not just after system boot).

I digged into the `/v1.0.0/libpod/info` , add some logs to see which function call cost most of the time.

during the stucking, `sudo podman info` also got stuck.

take the container `4206456a827d3492c6158a87ad2b487e48948b5b4cdc815791d58237fddee929` for example:

```
Jan 30 11:37:55 host001 podman[1643]: 2022-01-30 11:37:55.499825975 +0800 CST m=+541.880071243 container start 4206456a827d3492c6158a87ad2b487e48948b5b4cdc815791d58237fddee929 (image=docker.io/library/traefik:v2.6.0, name=traefik-210d1b7d-3163-1c7d-e02a-0709ef33314f, org.opencontainers.image.version=v2.6.0)
Jan 30 11:37:55 host001 podman[1643]: @ - - [30/Jan/2022:11:28:54 +0800] "POST /v1.0.0/libpod/containers/4206456a827d3492c6158a87ad2b487e48948b5b4cdc815791d58237fddee929/start HTTP/1.1" 204 0 "" "nomad-driver-podman/0.3.0"
J

Jan 30 11:37:56 host001 podman[1643]: time="2022-01-30T11:37:56+08:00" level=warning msg="xxxx: syncContainer UpdateContainer, cost_ms=849 id=4206456a827d3492c6158a87ad2b487e48948b5b4cdc815791d58237fddee929 name=traefik-210d1b7d-3163-1c7d-e02a-0709ef33314f"
Jan 30 11:37:56 host001 podman[1643]: time="2022-01-30T11:37:56+08:00" level=warning msg="xxxx: getContainerStoreInfo con.State, cost_ms=512585 id=4206456a827d3492c6158a87ad2b487e48948b5b4cdc815791d58237fddee929 name=traefik-210d1b7d-3163-1c7d-e02a-0709ef33314f"
```

in the call `func (r *Runtime) getContainerStoreInfo()`,  `func (c *Container) State()` took about `512585 ms`, that's about  `8 minutes`, but my log shows that `syncContainer()` only cost `849ms`. so the problem is `Require locking`:

```go
// State Accessors
// Require locking

// State returns the current state of the container
func (c *Container) State() (define.ContainerStatus, error) {
	if !c.batched {
		c.lock.Lock()
		defer c.lock.Unlock()

		if err := c.syncContainer(); err != nil {
			return define.ContainerStateUnknown, err
		}
	}
	return c.state.State, nil
}
```

## what did I do to fix up (a workaround) the problem ?

I make a change to `getContainerStoreInfo`, let it direct return to avoid `func (c *Container) State()` acquire locking problem.
and I tested again, the problem is gone.

the shortcoming is the `podman info` or the `/v1.0.0/libpod/info` api will always return empty containerStore counter like this:

```
containerStore:
    number: 0
    paused: 0
    running: 0
    stopped: 0
```

but this does not affect the real feature.

`r.GetAllContainers()` did not cost time, but `con.State()` have to acquire locking, I think this is the problem.

```go

func (r *Runtime) getContainerStoreInfo() (define.ContainerStore, error) {
	var (
		paused, running, stopped int
	)

	cs := define.ContainerStore{}
	
   ////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

	// direct return to avoid `func (c *Container) State()` acquire locking
	return cs, nil

   ////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

	cons, err := r.GetAllContainers()

	if err != nil {
		return cs, err
	}
	cs.Number = len(cons)
	for _, con := range cons {
		elapsed := ElapsedMilli()
		state, err := con.State()
		if err != nil {
			if errors.Cause(err) == define.ErrNoSuchCtr {
				// container was probably removed
				cs.Number--
				continue
			}
			return cs, err
		}
		switch state {
		case define.ContainerStateRunning:
			running++
		case define.ContainerStatePaused:
			paused++
		default:
			stopped++
		}
	}
	cs.Paused = paused
	cs.Stopped = stopped
	cs.Running = running
	return cs, nil
}
```


**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.  install podman latest version, [nomad](https://github.com/hashicorp/nomad/releases/tag/v1.2.4) v1.2.4, nomad-driver-podman (https://github.com/hashicorp/nomad-driver-podman)

2. start `podman.socket` , `nomad.service` 

3. using `nomad` start a lot of jobs using the nomad `podman` driver (like this https://github.com/hashicorp/nomad-driver-podman/blob/main/examples/jobs/redis_ports.nomad)
(for concurrent purpose)

4. ensure `podman.socket` , `nomad.service` both enabled at startup

5. reboot the system 

6. `sudo journalctl  -u podman --no-pager` and `sudo journalctl  -u nomad --no-pager`

**Describe the results you received:**

`nomad` stuck on startup  because the podman rest api stuck for a long time. after minutes, finally nomad started

**Describe the results you expected:**

`nomad` startup quickly (without stuck time, almost in seconds)

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

like I said about, maybe it is  `Require locking` problem.

comments.body:
Removing locking is not an option. It's absolutely essential for safe operation of Podman that each independent process be certain is has exclusive access to the container it's working on.

If there's a problem with locking, it's probably that another API operation is holding the required lock and is taking a very long time to do something - potentially container startup, though that normally shouldn't take long, as we only require the lock to send the start command, and the actual startup of the app in the container does not require locking.

Locking problem is what I guess. I'm not saying that removing the lock. podman db is based on bolt db, of course it requires locking.

But the thing is, if it is not startup on system reboot, there's no such issue.

the problem is, why concurrent call to conatiner start api, `libpod/info` api and container stats api got stuck.

Something is holding the locks in question for periods of up to 30 seconds, which is preventing `podman info` from acquiring the locks in question. I think we need to identify just what that is; from your description, it sounds like it would have to be either Start or Stats. Of the two, I think Start is more likely; something may be causing the OCI runtime to take far longer than we'd expect (I'd expect ~1 second maximum for that, even on a highly-loaded system; it does need to fork-exec the OCI runtime, but once that's done the actual start operation is very simple).
Also - is this new, as of Podman v4.0? Or did this happen with v3.x also?
I have a image with slow startup on my home server. Sometimes it takes up to 30 seconds to start the container but I never looked into it why it is that slow.

Currently it is v4.0

I'll try v3.x and then give the results.

podman v4.0

condition: leave the container in running state and then restart the system

this time I cleaned all exists container with `sudo podman rm -a`

and only start a redis container via podman api (via nomad driver podman).

and got the podman api server log.

the sitution is `nomad-driver-podman` try to start an exists container when nomad starts.
the  `nomad-driver-podman` has a 60s time limits on its api request. podman start the container exceeded 60s.
so  `nomad-driver-podman`  try to stop the container (because start failed), but podman is trying to start the container, and it is not done (dont' know why).  so the stop request will exceeded its 10s timeout (by config?). and then podman will send SIGKILL to the container. then the container got killed. `nomad-driver-podman`  confirmed the container died, so it try to force delete it.
after all succeeded, nomad startup done.  then it try to re-create the container, this time, it is done very quickly

what I did:

this time, `nomad-driver-podman` will not try to `start` the container again.

because when it get the container stats, podman gives it the status `created`,  not `exited`.

`nomad-driver-podman` will not try to `start` the container when the status is `exited`.

so the result is, `nomad-driver-podman`  re-create the container and then start it can be very fast.

Just tried with podman v3.4.4, the problem remains the same.

After system boot, the container start via the api stuck. (and in the same time if you try to run `sudo podman info`, it will stuck too).

After a more in-depth investigation, I finanlly got the reason.

When log collection option is enabled in nomad-driver-podman (which is default enabled),
the driver will create the container using a k8s-file path.
which result in the `conmon` command line have the params like:

```
-l k8s-file:/var/lib/nomad/alloc/462aaf3e-3e20-bfbb-990a-a5cbf5e5953c/alloc/logs/.redis.stdout.fifo
```
The file `.redis.stdout.fifo` is not a normal file, in fact it is a named fifo pipe.

but since the system rebooted, nomad is not started.

so there's no reader for `.redis.stdout.fifo`, but `conmon` try to open the named pipe in **blocking** mode,

which is, if there's no reader for the named pipe,  `conmon` will got stuck in waiting the file.

if the condition is not system reboot, `nomad-driver-podman` will start a steam log api call to read the log, which resulting podman api handler is reading the log file. so it does has reader. conmon can startup without stucking.

but if it is just after system reboot, the nomad process is starting.
it find a `exited` container (which managed by nomad podman driver), first it try to start it and then it will start reading its log.

so here is the problem.

it is `nomad-driver-podman`  who tirgger `conmon` to read the named pipe, the `nomad-driver-podman`  will not read (or trigger podman api to read) the named pipe.

so `conmon` got stuck forever (until there's a reader or podman api client exceeded its timeout and delete the container).

but, there's also other problems.

I think it has effect on normal file too. according to https://www.gnu.org/software/libc/manual/html_node/Open_002dtime-Flags.html#index-O_005fNONBLOCK

> Macro: int `O_NONBLOCK`

> This prevents open from blocking for a “long time” to open the file. This is only meaningful for some kinds of files, usually devices such as serial ports; when it is not meaningful, it is harmless and ignored. Often, opening a port to a modem blocks until the modem reports carrier detection; if O_NONBLOCK is specified, open will return immediately without a carrier.
> 
> Note that the O_NONBLOCK flag is overloaded as **both** an I/O operating mode and a file name translation flag. This means that specifying O_NONBLOCK in open also sets nonblocking I/O mode; see [Operating Modes](https://www.gnu.org/software/libc/manual/html_node/Operating-Modes.html). To open the file without blocking but do normal I/O that blocks, you must call open with O_NONBLOCK set and then call fcntl to turn the bit off.

The important one is:
> To open the file without blocking but do normal I/O that blocks, you must call `open` with `O_NONBLOCK` set and then call `fcntl` to turn the bit off.

but, if log file is a named pipe and there's no reader currently,  with `O_NONBLOCK` set, `conmon` will fail very fast with an error "Failed to open log file". (see https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/13081#issuecomment-1028723328)
